title: btc-graph content source audit
objective: Audit Bitcoin educational sources and expand btc-graph with atomic concepts and strict prerequisite edges.
notes: Canonical source of truth for the content discovery loop. Update this JSON first, then regenerate the workbook.

sources.name: Bitcoin Optech Topics
sources.scope: Topic index covering protocol, wallet, Lightning, fee, relay, and operational concepts.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the index views plus individual topic pages for canonical naming, aliases, see-also links, and primary docs.
sources.notes: 157 topics plus 117 aliases make this a naming registry and gap detector more than a bulk node source. Implemented from this source in the current branch: protocol.x-only-public-keys, protocol.version-3-transaction-relay, protocol.tapscript, dev.taproot-psbt-fields, protocol.accidental-confiscation, dev.taproot-descriptors, dev.descriptor-wallet-policies, and dev.descriptors-in-psbt.

sources.name: BTC Transcripts Topics
sources.scope: Topic index into transcript-backed technical discussions and workshops.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track Topics, Categories, Types, and Sources as separate provenance surfaces before drilling into individual transcript families.
sources.notes: Use mainly for provenance and terminology normalization. Topic labels should not be imported 1:1.

sources.name: Bitcoin Dev Philosophy
sources.scope: Conceptual and systems-thinking source likely to surface bridge concepts and framing gaps.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track 9 chapter-level rows first, with later splits for dense chapters 5, 7, 8, and 9.
sources.notes: Heavy overlap with existing philosophy nodes, but strong for bridge concepts like validation sovereignty, consensus vs policy, and development review. Implemented from this source in the current branch: fundamentals.validation-sovereignty, protocol.consensus-vs-policy, protocol.chain-split-risk, and dev.review-culture.

sources.name: Mastering Bitcoin Book Map
sources.scope: Structured chapter map for foundational and implementation-oriented concept coverage.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track 14 chapter rows first, grouping chapters 4 to 12 as the primary extraction surface.
sources.notes: Already heavily reused in current nodes, so chapter titles are containers. Best current value is prerequisite ladders and bridge concepts. Implemented from this source in the current branch: protocol.light-client-trust-model, ops.full-node-vs-lightweight-wallet, custody.coin-selection, protocol.change-outputs, protocol.bech32m-addresses, protocol.script-execution-stack, dev.extended-keys, protocol.witness-structure, and protocol.partial-merkle-proofs.

sources.name: Optech Schnorr/Taproot Workshop
sources.scope: Focused advanced curriculum around Schnorr, Taproot, and related prerequisite chains.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the 12 module pages plus shared assets once per source.
sources.notes: Best current source for missing Taproot bridge concepts such as tagged hashes, taptweak, key-path spends, tapleaf inclusion proofs, Huffman-optimized TapTrees, and concrete policy case studies like degrading multisig. Implemented from this source in the current branch: protocol.tagged-hashes, protocol.taptweak, protocol.taproot-key-path-spends, protocol.taptree, protocol.tapleaf-inclusion-proofs, protocol.huffman-optimized-taptrees, and custody.degrading-multisig. Current tracked workshop queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Decoding by Bitcoin Devs
sources.scope: Structured educational material that may reveal curriculum-style prerequisite bundles.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the Introduction, Tools, Transactions, Scripts, and Taproot module groups before lesson-level expansion.
sources.notes: Best used for sequencing and beginner-friendly prerequisite ordering rather than broad topic discovery. Implemented from this source in the current branch: protocol.p2pk, security.nonce-reuse-attack, protocol.p2pkh, protocol.fee-calculation, protocol.op-checkmultisig, protocol.p2ms, protocol.bare-multisig-standardness, and protocol.musig. Current tracked module queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Programming Lightning
sources.scope: Interactive Lightning curriculum covering payment channels, channel state updates, HTLCs, onion routing, routing economics, and visual Lightning simulations.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the Lightning Tutorial, Onion Routing Tutorial, and Visual Lightning surfaces as separate course clusters before drilling into individual lessons.
sources.notes: Added from user direction. The public app bundle clearly exposes Lightning Tutorial, Onion Routing Tutorial, and Visual Lightning as the main extraction surfaces, which should produce bridge concepts in the payment-channel and routing branches without depending on transcript-style provenance. Implemented from this source in the current branch: lightning.per-commitment-secrets, lightning.obscured-commitment-number, lightning.commitment-output-scripts, and lightning.htlc-output-scripts. Current tracked surface queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Lightning Network BOLTs
sources.scope: Canonical Lightning wire protocol, transport, channel, transaction, routing, discovery, feature-negotiation, invoice, and offer specifications.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track each BOLT document as an enumeration cluster, starting with the index and BOLTs 01, 02, 04, 08, 09, and 10. Extract only atomic mechanisms and deduplicate against existing Lightning nodes before proposing additions.
sources.notes: Added during source expansion. The first inventory covers the BOLT index plus messaging, peer protocol, onion messaging, encrypted transport, feature negotiation, and DNS bootstrap clusters. Implemented from this source in the current branch: lightning.feature-flags, lightning.onion-messages, lightning.encrypted-authenticated-transport, and lightning.dns-seeds.

sources.name: Bitcoin Improvement Proposals
sources.scope: Canonical Bitcoin Improvement Proposals covering process, consensus rules, signatures, wallet formats, applications, peer services, relay, APIs, mining, and deployment mechanisms.
sources.clusterStrategy: Enumerate the repository registry first, then group BIPs into consensus soft forks, signature encoding and serialization, script and output templates, wallet and payment standards, and peer/relay protocols. Treat BIPs as specification provenance rather than automatic one-to-one nodes.
sources.notes: Added during source expansion. Initial inventory covers the registry/process, consensus soft forks, signature encoding and serialization, script/output templates, wallet/payment standards, and peer/relay clusters. Implemented from this source in the current branch: protocol.strict-der-signatures, custody.deterministic-multisig-key-sorting, and protocol.generic-signed-messages.

sources.name: Mempool.space Documentation
sources.scope: Operational documentation for fee visibility, mempool behavior, transaction monitoring, APIs, and self-hosting. Treat it as implementation and observability guidance, not consensus authority.
sources.clusterStrategy: Enumerate the FAQ, REST API, WebSocket API, and self-hosting/repository clusters first. Use Bitcoin Core documentation as the canonical authority for consensus and policy semantics, then retain Mempool.space-specific observability concepts only when they survive deduplication.
sources.notes: Added during source expansion. Initial clusters cover FAQ/mempool behavior, REST API, WebSocket/live events, and self-hosting. Implemented from this source in the current branch: ops.mempool-purging, with Bitcoin Core policy and RPC documentation used as canonical supporting sources.

sources.name: Cypherpunk Library Bitcoin-Adjacent Canon
sources.scope: Bitcoin-adjacent cypherpunk readings focused on privacy, electronic cash, monetary theory, and the ideological roots most likely to sharpen foundational and history nodes.
sources.clusterStrategy: Start with four clusters: cypherpunk manifestos, privacy and surveillance essays, eCash and digital-cash texts, and monetary-theory readings. Defer clearly non-Bitcoin-adjacent political or general-hacker texts unless they surface a direct graph gap.
sources.notes: Added from user direction. High-signal starting texts from the collection include A Cypherpunk's Manifesto, The Crypto Anarchist Manifesto, Your Secret Right to Cash, Protecting Privacy with Electronic Cash, The Beauty of eCash, The Cyphernomicon, Ideal Money and Asymptotically Ideal Money, and 21 Lessons. Implemented from this source in the current branch: history.chaumian-ecash, privacy.secret-right-to-cash, economics.ideal-money. Merged into existing coverage in the current branch: history.cypherpunks via A Cypherpunk's Manifesto; history.bitcoin-anarchism via The Crypto Anarchist Manifesto. Current tracked cluster queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Bitcoin Dev Project Explained Posts
sources.scope: Short-form educational posts from @Bitcoin_Devs that explicitly explain protocol, wallet, and Lightning mechanisms in learner-facing language.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the live X search as one source-family row first, then expand to one row per matching post once current results are enumerated. Group matching posts by concept family such as hashing, fee management, node architecture, channel mechanics, and upgrade proposals.
sources.notes: Added from user direction. The live X query was enumerated in the logged-in in-app browser on July 8, 2026 and surfaced 43 matching posts. Visible examples include Lamport Signature explained, Duplicate transaction bug explained, 64-byte transaction bug explained, SegWit Address explained, Off-by-one difficulty bug explained, Great Consensus Cleanup explained Part 1: Time Warp Attack, Transaction Pinning explained, Full Node Architecture Explained, MAST explained simply, BIP 158 explained, MuSig explained Simply, CPFP transaction explained, CISA explained, BIP-68 Explained, and Shamir's Secret Sharing explained. Implemented from this source in the current branch: protocol.time-warp-attack, protocol.duplicate-transaction-bug, protocol.64-byte-transaction-bug, fundamentals.lamport-signatures, custody.shamirs-secret-sharing. Merged into existing coverage in the current branch: protocol.time-warp-attack via Off-by-one difficulty bug explained. Current enumerated post queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Bitcoin Core Academy Wallet Internals
sources.scope: Wallet internals cluster around ScriptPubKeyManagers, wallet keys, transaction identification, and balance calculation.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track one wallet-internals page cluster at a time so Core-specific abstractions stay atomic instead of collapsing into generic wallet concepts.
sources.notes: First additional-source pass beyond the mandatory seed list. The current additive winner is dev.scriptpubkeymanagers; wallet-encryption remains deferred because the cleanest surviving slice is still too coupled to missing Core wallet-internals parents. Current tracked wallet-internals queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Bitcoin Core Academy Mempool Mechanics
sources.scope: Mempool and relay internals cluster around transaction relay, rebroadcast behavior, and local node transaction state.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the academy mempool pages as a separate cluster so implementation-specific relay mechanisms are not collapsed into generic mempool coverage.
sources.notes: Second post-seed-source slice. The current additive winners are protocol.mempool-unbroadcast-set, protocol.transaction-rebroadcast-privacy, and protocol.txid-vs-wtxid. The remaining transaction-format internals are now mostly implementation-detail material rather than clean learner-facing nodes. Current tracked mempool queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Bitcoin Design Payment Request Formats
sources.scope: User-facing payment request and address-sharing concepts across human-readable addresses, transaction display, and wallet privacy tradeoffs.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track design guide pages by UX concept cluster so user-facing payment abstractions stay separate from protocol internals and generic wallet hygiene.
sources.notes: First Bitcoin Design implementation slice. The current additive winner is custody.human-readable-addresses; encrypted cloud backups remains the main deferred neighbor from this design source family. Current tracked payment-format queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Bitcoin Design Wallet Activity Context
sources.scope: User-facing wallet activity, contacts, and contextual payment-history concepts that help learners distinguish on-chain events from wallet-added meaning.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track activity and contacts pages together so contextual metadata stays separate from backup flows and from protocol transaction mechanics.
sources.notes: Second Bitcoin Design implementation slice. The current additive winner is custody.transaction-metadata; encrypted cloud backups remains the main deferred design-side candidate. Current tracked activity-and-contacts queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Bitcoin Design Multiple Wallets
sources.scope: User-facing wallet architecture around separating funds by purpose, ownership, and security model inside one application.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the multiple-wallets page separately so purpose-based wallet separation stays distinct from account-path mechanics, backup flows, and generic wallet activity metadata.
sources.notes: Third Bitcoin Design implementation slice. The current additive winner is custody.multiple-wallets; encrypted cloud backups remains deferred because it overlaps too heavily with backups-recovery. Current tracked page queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Bitcoin Design Automatic Cloud Backup
sources.scope: User-facing backup scheme that stores encrypted wallet recovery data with an OS or cloud provider for low-friction restoration.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the conceptual cloud-backup page, the daily-spending reference flow, and the private-key-management overview together so the scheme can be deduped against existing backup and Lightning recovery coverage.
sources.notes: Reviewed and merged into existing coverage instead of minting a node. The enduring source value is backup-scheme tradeoffs that already fit custody.backups-recovery plus lightning.channel-backups.

sources.name: Bitcoin Design Upgradeable Wallet
sources.scope: User-facing wallet architecture for progressive security and staged upgrades between key-management schemes.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the upgradeable-wallet reference design separately so progressive security becomes its own concept instead of staying buried inside cloud-backup discussions or multiwallet archival flows.
sources.notes: Fourth Bitcoin Design implementation slice candidate. The current additive winner is custody.progressive-security, which survives dedupe more cleanly than automatic cloud backups or wallet-encryption. Current tracked page queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Bitcoin Design Shared Wallet
sources.scope: Reference design for co-managed wallets using multi-key schemes, provider-held recovery keys, and household approval policies.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the shared-wallet page together with the generic multi-key explanation so persona-specific multisig scenarios are deduped against existing custody mechanism nodes.
sources.notes: Reviewed and merged into existing coverage instead of minting a node. The enduring source value is shared-wallet examples for custody.multisig, custody.multiple-wallets, and custody.progressive-security.

sources.name: Bitcoin Developer Guides
sources.scope: Canonical developer-facing guide sections covering block chain, transactions, contracts, wallets, payment processing, operating modes, P2P network, and mining.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the eight top-level guide sections first, then split dense sections like Transactions, Contracts, Wallets, and P2P Network when they expose concept gaps not already covered by existing nodes.
sources.notes: Added after the current audited source queue was exhausted. The first pass across the top-level sections surfaced four clean candidates: protocol.bitcoin-uri-bip21 from Payment Processing, dev.hardened-keys from Wallets, protocol.escrow-and-arbitration from Contracts, and mining.pool-shares from Pool Mining. A follow-up Wallet Files pass surfaced dev.wallet-import-format-wif as another clean wallet-standard slice. The implemented winners are now protocol.bitcoin-uri-bip21, dev.hardened-keys, protocol.escrow-and-arbitration, dev.wallet-import-format-wif, and mining.pool-shares because they land as missing standards, key-derivation, contract-pattern, key-serialization, and pooled-mining accounting bridges with clean direct parents. The current tracked top-level-plus-Wallet-Files queue is exhausted for this pass, though future deeper section splits may still surface more concepts.

sources.name: Bitcoin Core Operator Documentation
sources.scope: Bitcoin Core's operator-facing documentation and configuration references for securing node control surfaces, especially JSON-RPC exposure and credentials.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the JSON-RPC interface security section, bitcoin.conf reference, and implementation configuration paths as one operator-security cluster before splitting distinct access-control or privacy concepts.
sources.notes: Added after the audited queue surfaced a distinct RPC control-plane security gap. The first inspected cluster covers credentials, rpcbind/rpcallowip exposure, and the implementation path that applies these settings.

sources.name: Bitcoin Core Package Policy and Admission Documentation
sources.scope: Bitcoin Core's package mempool acceptance rules, submitpackage RPC, and release history for local package admission and fee-bumping behavior.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the current submitpackage RPC reference, package-policy specification, and the 26.0 release note that introduced the RPC as one package-admission cluster; keep network package relay and cluster-mempool as deduplicated neighboring concepts.
sources.notes: Added after the RPC-security slice. The inspected pages establish submitpackage as a local package-admission mechanism and package RBF as a distinct replacement rule, separate from testmempoolaccept preflight, network package relay, and cluster-mempool internals.

sources.name: Bitcoin Core Cluster Mempool and RPC Documentation
sources.scope: Bitcoin Core's cluster-mempool data model, bounded cluster policy, and related RPCs for inspecting connected transactions, mining order, chunks, fees, weights, and whole-mempool fee curves.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the getmempoolcluster and getmempoolfeeratediagram RPC references, cluster-limit policy, Bitcoin Core 31.0 release notes, mempool terminology, and implementation paths as one cluster-mempool slice; keep concrete RPC workflows merged into the higher-level concepts.
sources.notes: Added after the package-RBF slice. The inspected documentation exposes two RPC implementation surfaces, both merged into protocol.cluster-mempool, plus a distinct learner-facing policy concept: bounded cluster limits replacing legacy ancestor/descendant limits.

concepts.title: Tagged Hashes
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 0.3 Tagged Hashes
concepts.atomicityRationale: A narrow cryptographic construction repeatedly used in Taproot and Schnorr material, distinct from generic hash functions.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the base hash-function mental model before namespace-separated tagged hashing makes sense.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: X-Only Public Keys
concepts.sourceContext: Topics index and Taproot-related topic pages
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a specific key representation concept that bridges Schnorr math to Taproot output keys.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The concept depends on both general key knowledge and the Schnorr/Taproot context in which x-only keys matter.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: TapTweak
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 2.2 Taptweak
concepts.atomicityRationale: A concrete Taproot mechanism that is smaller than Taproot as a whole and useful for gap diagnosis.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The tweak mechanism is a Taproot-specific key derivation step that directly depends on Taproot context, tagged hashing, and x-only key encoding.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: Taproot Key-Path Spends
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop modules 2.0 Introduction to Taproot and 2.1 Segwit V1
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific Taproot spending mode: spending by signature against the tweaked output key without revealing a script path. It is narrower than Taproot overall, narrower than TapTweak as a key-derivation mechanism, and distinct from Tapscript's script-path execution rules.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The direct learner need is understanding that the spend authorizes against the tweaked Taproot output key. Schnorr signatures and the broader Taproot context are already inherited through TapTweak, while script-path concepts such as Tapscript and TapTree are sibling alternatives rather than prerequisites.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest remaining workshop-native Taproot spending-mode slice after ruling out broader wrappers and keeping script-path detail in the Tapscript and TapTree branches.

concepts.title: TapTree
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 2.4 Taptree
concepts.atomicityRationale: Specific Taproot script-tree structure, distinct from generic Merkle trees and from Taproot overall.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: TapTree is best taught immediately after TapTweak. Merkle-tree knowledge remains useful context but is already reached transitively through the Taproot path, so the direct edge would be redundant.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: TapLeaf Inclusion Proofs
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 2.4 Taptree
concepts.atomicityRationale: A concrete proof object within script-path spends, narrower than Taproot and narrower than TapTree.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The proof only makes sense after the learner understands the TapTree structure it proves against.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: Huffman-Optimized TapTrees
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 2.5 Huffman Construction
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a narrow optimization concept that explains one specific design tradeoff inside Taproot trees.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The optimization is about arranging TapTree leaves, so the base tree concept is direct.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the last explicit pending workshop optimization concept.

concepts.title: Degrading Multisig
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 3.1 Degrading Multisig
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific timeout-based custody policy, narrower than generic multisig, narrower than threshold signatures, and narrower than broad inheritance or recovery planning.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the baseline multisig model and timelock semantics first. Taproot is a clean implementation path surfaced by the workshop, but the custody policy itself can be understood before committing to a specific script-path mechanism. Generic backup planning is a downstream use context rather than a prerequisite for understanding this script-policy construction.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the next workshop-derived custody case study, while leaving broader threshold-signature umbrella material for later and keeping Taproot as implementation context rather than a direct prerequisite.

concepts.title: Chain Split Risk
concepts.sourceContext: Upgrading chapter 5.3.1 Costs of a split
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific upgrade-risk concept about the costs of incompatible rule sets continuing in parallel, not a broad deployment-mechanisms umbrella.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the compatibility distinction between soft forks and hard forks before the consequences of a persistent split make sense.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a narrow upgrade-risk bridge between protocol.softfork-hardfork and the existing activation-history case studies.

concepts.title: Tapscript
concepts.sourceContext: Topics alias normalization around Tapscript and Taproot script-path rules
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is the specific Taproot-era script ruleset, narrower than Taproot and cleaner than a broad script-path-spends explainer.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The direct learner need is the Taproot spend context. The base Script model is already inherited transitively through the SegWit and transaction-lifecycle path, so adding protocol.script directly would be redundant in the current graph.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the next additive Taproot bridge without rewiring older phase-locked descendants such as protocol.op-checksigadd.

concepts.title: Taproot PSBT Fields
concepts.sourceContext: PSBT topic page plus linked implementation notes around BIP371 support for Taproot inputs and outputs
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is the narrow PSBT extension surface for Taproot-specific fields, distinct from general PSBT workflows, descriptors, and MuSig-specific coordination formats.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the base PSBT exchange model and the Taproot spend model first. A direct dev.psbt-v2 edge would overfit the node because BIP371 extends both PSBTv0 and PSBTv2 rather than depending on v2 specifically.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a narrow wallet-development bridge between generic PSBT workflows and Taproot-specific signing metadata.

concepts.title: Accidental Confiscation
concepts.sourceContext: Optech topic page on soft-fork designs that can accidentally make old funds unspendable
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific upgrade-risk failure mode about spendability loss under new soft-fork restrictions, not a broad activation or governance wrapper.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the soft-fork restriction model before this failure mode makes sense. The chain-split and standardness-policy branches add useful context but are not direct prerequisites for the core concept.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a narrow upgrade-risk leaf after protocol.chain-split-risk, without forcing policy or activation-history rewires.

concepts.title: Coin Selection
concepts.sourceContext: Chapter 2 overview: making change and coin selection during wallet transaction construction
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific wallet decision layer about choosing which UTXOs fund a payment, narrower than wallet management and broader than any one privacy heuristic or RPC surface.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The direct learner need is fee pressure and transaction-size tradeoffs. The UTXO model is already inherited transitively through the fee-market branch, and manual coin control is a downstream application of coin selection rather than a prerequisite.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a wallet-construction bridge that parents several existing privacy and funding leaves conceptually, without rewiring those older phase-locked nodes yet.

concepts.title: Change Outputs
concepts.sourceContext: Chapter 2 wallet transaction construction flow: making change before coin selection and later privacy heuristics
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific transaction-construction concept: returning excess input value to the spender as a new output. It is narrower than generic outputs and narrower than the later wallet-policy and privacy decisions built on top of it.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the basic output model first. Transaction-lifecycle is broader context already inherited in the output branch, and coin selection is a downstream wallet decision that depends on understanding change outputs rather than preceding it.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the missing bridge between addresses-outputs and the existing coin-selection and change-privacy leaves.

concepts.title: Taproot Descriptors
concepts.sourceContext: Output script descriptors topic page plus BIP386 tr() descriptor support for P2TR outputs
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates the tr() descriptor layer for Taproot outputs, narrower than generic descriptors and narrower than PSBT or MuSig workflow integrations.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the base descriptor grammar and the Taproot output model before Taproot-specific descriptor structure makes sense. Import workflows are downstream consumers rather than prerequisites.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a clean descriptor-side Taproot leaf without reopening PSBT transport or MuSig workflow rewires.

concepts.title: Descriptor Wallet Policies
concepts.sourceContext: Output script descriptors topic page mention of BIP388 wallet policies for descriptor wallets, reinforced by the HWI topic's external-signer use of descriptors
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates the BIP388 wallet-policy abstraction, narrower than generic descriptor wallets and distinct from descriptor import or PSBT signing flows.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the descriptor grammar and HD key-derivation structure first. Import flows and PSBT transport are downstream consumers of the wallet-policy format rather than prerequisites for understanding the policy abstraction itself.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the clean BIP388 slice after concluding that the broader descriptor-wallets label would duplicate the existing descriptor-wallet-imports alias.

concepts.title: Descriptors in PSBTs
concepts.sourceContext: PSBT topic page plus output script descriptors topic updates about descriptor-aware signer and wallet coordination
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one interoperability concept: carrying descriptor information inside PSBT-based coordination flows, narrower than generic PSBT workflows and narrower than broad descriptor-wallet abstractions.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the descriptor model and the base PSBT coordination model first. PSBT v2 and wallet-policy formats are important adjacent evolutions, but they are downstream refinements rather than prerequisites for the general descriptor-in-PSBT idea.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the cleanest remaining Optech-side interoperability slice after ruling out MAST, threshold signatures, and scriptless multisignatures as broader or more duplicate-prone.

concepts.title: MuSig
concepts.sourceContext: Taproot module page MuSig, reinforced by the Optech MuSig topic page and related BTC Transcripts discussions
concepts.atomicityRationale: MuSig is one specific aggregated-signature protocol, narrower than generic threshold signatures and narrower than downstream MuSig2 descriptor workflows.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the Schnorr signature model first. Wallet-level multisig and Taproot are useful comparison or deployment contexts, but they are not required to make the core aggregation concept intelligible.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the next shared Decoding, Optech, and BTC Transcripts slice without broadening into threshold-signature umbrella material or MuSig2-specific wallet rewires.

concepts.title: Version 3 Transaction Relay
concepts.sourceContext: Topics alias normalization around TRUC and version 3 relay
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a distinct relay-policy concept that should not be collapsed into the broader package relay bucket.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The core learner need is to understand the pinning problem that v3 relay is trying to constrain. In the current graph, the policy boundary is already inherited transitively through the transaction-pinning branch, so adding it directly would be redundant.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the next policy-leaf slice after adding protocol.consensus-vs-policy. Alias normalization remains mandatory here because Optech names and implementation jargon vary, and the direct policy-boundary edge was intentionally dropped after the redundancy check proved it was already inherited.

concepts.title: Validation Sovereignty
concepts.sourceContext: Trustlessness chapter and dont trust verify framing
concepts.atomicityRationale: A precise learner outcome about why running or relying on validation matters, not just a broad philosophy label.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: It is a narrower applied consequence of trustlessness and verification culture.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the philosophy-side bridge between trustlessness and practical self-validation.

concepts.title: Consensus vs Policy
concepts.sourceContext: Upgrade and split-risk discussion, reinforced by policy-heavy material in Mastering Bitcoin
concepts.atomicityRationale: A precise distinction that explains multiple later topics like relay, fee bumping, and upgrades.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the base idea of consensus rules before contrasting them with local relay policy.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch after the Taproot-internal slice. This is the cleanest conceptual parent for the existing policy and relay cluster.

concepts.title: Address Reuse Harms
concepts.sourceContext: Wallet, privacy, and address-format clusters; reinforced by philosophy privacy chapter
concepts.atomicityRationale: Narrow user-facing privacy consequence that is more actionable than a broad privacy umbrella.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The harm is best understood once the learner knows what addresses are and why pseudonymity is fragile.
concepts.notes: Do not add as a separate node. Fold any improved copy or resources into privacy.address-reuse.

concepts.title: Timelock Taxonomy
concepts.sourceContext: Authorization and transaction chapters covering CLTV, CSV, and sequence-based relative timelocks
concepts.atomicityRationale: A useful bridge concept that organizes multiple already-existing timelock mechanisms into one mental model.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The taxonomy should hang off the existing general timelock node rather than duplicate specific mechanism nodes.
concepts.notes: Do not add as a separate node. Enrich protocol.timelocks to make the taxonomy more explicit.

concepts.title: Light Client Trust Model
concepts.sourceContext: Network and blockchain chapters covering SPV, compact block filters, and lightweight-client tradeoffs
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a specific tradeoff concept that bridges full-node verification to lightweight client behavior.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The trust model only makes sense once the learner can contrast full-node guarantees with filtered-client shortcuts.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a thin conceptual bridge without rewiring older phase-locked neighbor nodes.

concepts.title: Full Node vs Lightweight Wallet
concepts.sourceContext: Introduction chapter discussion of full-node versus lightweight operating modes
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a concrete learner decision point with one clear outcome: understand how wallet mode changes verification guarantees and trust assumptions.

concepts.title: Transaction Witness Structure
concepts.sourceContext: Transactions > Witness Structure > Witness Structure Serialization
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific transaction-format concept: how each input carries a witness stack and how the marker, flag, item counts, and witness items are serialized. It is narrower than SegWit as an upgrade and distinct from the identifier and block-commitment consequences of witness data.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: SegWit is the direct conceptual change that introduces witness serialization. TXID/WTXID and witness commitments are downstream consequences or validation applications, so adding them directly would create redundant or backward-facing edges.
concepts.notes: Implemented as the missing transaction-level SegWit bridge. Existing TXID/WTXID and witness-commitment nodes remain unchanged until a separate prerequisite-cleanup pass evaluates whether they should consume this node.

concepts.title: Partial Merkle Proofs
concepts.sourceContext: The Blockchain > Merkle Trees and Lightweight Clients
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific client-verification mechanism: sending only the relevant Merkle branches and traversal bits needed to prove a transaction belongs to a block. It is narrower than Merkle trees, block structure, and the broader light-client trust model, and distinct from compact block filters.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Block structure supplies the Merkle-root context and already inherits the generic Merkle-tree concept. The light-client trust model is a downstream interpretation of what this proof can and cannot verify, so making it a direct prerequisite would reverse the mechanism-to-tradeoff relationship.
concepts.notes: Implemented as the missing legacy-SPV proof mechanism between block structure and the existing light-client trust model. Compact block filters remain a distinct modern client-sync approach rather than a duplicate.

concepts.title: Review Culture in Bitcoin Development
concepts.sourceContext: Open Source chapter > Review
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific development-process concept: how independent review across proposal, implementation, deployment, and adoption stages reduces correctness, security, and compatibility risk. It is narrower than the BIPs process and distinct from general adversarial thinking, reproducible builds, and responsible disclosure.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The proposal process supplies the lifecycle in which review occurs, while adversarial thinking supplies the threat-driven review mindset. Reproducible builds and responsible disclosure are specialized downstream safeguards rather than prerequisites for understanding review culture itself.
concepts.notes: Implemented as a narrow Bitcoin-development review concept. Permissionless development remains deferred because it overlaps more heavily with neutrality, censorship resistance, and the BIPs process.

concepts.title: Lightning Feature Flags
concepts.sourceContext: BOLT 09: Features > feature-bit registry and optional/required semantics
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific interoperability mechanism: capability bits that implementations advertise and negotiate across init messages, announcements, invoices, and related protocol contexts. It is narrower than Lightning gossip, peer messaging, and the individual protocols that consume the negotiated features.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The gossip protocol supplies the Lightning network-advertisement context in which feature signaling is used. P2P messages and routing are inherited implementation or consumer context rather than direct prerequisites for understanding the feature-bit negotiation mechanism.
concepts.notes: Implemented as the first BOLTs source-expansion slice. The odd/even optional-versus-required rule is kept inside this node rather than split into separate feature-bit nodes.

concepts.title: Lightning Onion Messages
concepts.sourceContext: BOLT 04: Onion Routing Protocol > Onion Messages
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific Lightning transport mechanism: forwarding unreliable encrypted messages over blinded onion paths without using a payment HTLC. It is distinct from payment onion routing, route blinding itself, and BOLT12 offer encoding.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Feature negotiation explains how peers signal onion-message support, and route blinding explains the privacy-preserving path construction. Payment onion routing is a sibling transport model, while BOLT12 offers are one downstream message use case rather than a prerequisite.
concepts.notes: Implemented as the next BOLTs slice after feature flags. BOLT 08 transport, BOLT 10 DNS bootstrap, and already-covered dual funding remain queued or merge/defer candidates.

concepts.title: Lightning Encrypted and Authenticated Transport
concepts.sourceContext: BOLT 08: Encrypted and Authenticated Transport > Noise_XK handshake, ECDH key derivation, and ChaCha20-Poly1305 framed transport
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one Lightning wire-transport mechanism: authenticating peer identities, deriving session keys, and encrypting framed messages. It is narrower than generic cryptographic transport and distinct from Bitcoin's BIP324 P2P transport.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Public/private keys supply the long-term identity model and Diffie-Hellman supplies the shared-secret mechanism. Bitcoin P2P v2 transport is a sibling protocol with different peer and wire-context requirements, not a prerequisite for BOLT 08.
concepts.notes: Selected as the cleanest remaining P0 BOLTs concept after deduping against protocol.v2-p2p-transport. Keep the Lightning-specific Noise_XK and framing outcome visible rather than collapsing both transports into a generic encryption node.

concepts.title: Strict DER Signature Encoding
concepts.sourceContext: BIP 66: Strict DER Signatures
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific deployed consensus rule: legacy ECDSA signatures must use strict DER encoding. It is narrower than digital signatures, Script, and transaction malleability, and it isolates a concrete validation behavior rather than a broad BIP or soft-fork umbrella.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The Script node already inherits the digital-signature verification path in this graph, so it is the only direct parent needed for strict encoding checks. Transaction malleability is related historical motivation, not a necessary direct parent for understanding the rule itself.
concepts.notes: Implemented as the first BIPs source-expansion slice. BIP 66 is treated as a specific consensus validation rule, not as a duplicate of transaction malleability or a generic soft-fork node. The direct edge is kept only on protocol.script because the digital-signature path is already inherited.

concepts.title: Deterministic Multisig Key Sorting
concepts.sourceContext: BIP 67 > deterministic sorting of compressed public keys in multisig redeem scripts
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one interoperability convention: sorting compressed public keys before constructing a multisig script. It is narrower than multisig generally and distinct from P2SH, which supplies the script-hash wrapper rather than the signer-ordering rule.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the signer-policy model and the P2SH script-hash context before the deterministic key-ordering convention is useful for wallet interoperability. The shared Script ancestor is inherited and should not be added directly.
concepts.notes: Selected from the BIPs wallet/payment standards cluster after deduping against multisig itself, P2SH, and broader wallet address conventions.

concepts.title: Mempool Purging and Rolling Minimum Fee
concepts.sourceContext: FAQ > stuck transactions, mempool fullness, purging threshold, and rolling minimum fee
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific node-operations behavior: how mempool pressure raises a rolling minimum relay feerate and causes lower-feerate transactions to be evicted. It is narrower than mempool policy, fee markets, package relay, and generic transaction confirmation.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Mempool policy is the direct conceptual parent for local relay thresholds and eviction. The generic mempool and fee-market concepts are already inherited through that policy path, so adding them directly would be redundant or too broad.
concepts.notes: Implemented as the first mempool.space source-expansion slice, with Bitcoin Core policy and getmempoolinfo documentation anchoring the semantics.

concepts.title: Generic Signed Message Format
concepts.sourceContext: BIP 322: Generic Signed Message Format
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one interoperability format: proving that a signer can satisfy a Bitcoin scriptPubKey for a message through virtual transactions. It is narrower than Script, digital signatures, PSBT, Taproot, and Scriptless Scripts because those describe the execution substrate, signing workflow, script variants, or broader construction family rather than the generic message format itself.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: BIP 322 is defined around satisfying an output script, so Script is the only direct prerequisite. Digital signatures are already inherited through the Script branch, while PSBT, Schnorr, and Taproot are variant-specific or related signing contexts rather than requirements for understanding the generic format.
concepts.notes: Added after deduping against Script, digital signatures, PSBT, Taproot, OP_CHECKSIGFROMSTACK, and the cross-domain Scriptless Scripts umbrella.

concepts.title: Bitcoin Core RPC Access Security
concepts.sourceContext: JSON-RPC Interface > Security, bitcoin.conf RPC settings, and init.cpp configuration handling
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one operational security boundary: safely exposing Bitcoin Core's powerful RPC control plane through authentication and network-access limits. It is narrower than the generic RPC interface and distinct from wallet RPC workflows, transaction preflight, package relay, and general node security.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the RPC interface and its authority before its access boundary can make sense. Full-node operation is already inherited through dev.bitcoin-core-rpc, and adversarial thinking is useful context but not a direct technical requirement for understanding authentication and exposure controls.
concepts.notes: Added after deduping against dev.bitcoin-core-rpc, dev.testmempoolaccept-rpc, wallet RPC nodes, package relay, and general node-security concepts.

concepts.title: submitpackage RPC
concepts.sourceContext: submitpackage RPC reference, package-policy specification, and Bitcoin Core 26.0 release notes
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one developer-facing operation: submitting a transaction package to a local node for package-aware mempool admission and reading the result. It is narrower than package relay, cluster mempool, and package policy generally, and distinct from testmempoolaccept because it attempts local admission rather than only preflighting the package.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the existing RPC preflight workflow before the distinction between testing and submitting a package is useful. Package relay is a network-propagation layer, cluster mempool is an internal policy design, and CPFP is a motivating use case rather than a direct technical requirement.
concepts.notes: Added after deduping against dev.testmempoolaccept-rpc, protocol.package-relay, protocol.cluster-mempool, and package-CPFP concepts.

concepts.title: Package RBF
concepts.sourceContext: Package RBF and 1P1C-topology package replacement in Bitcoin Core policy and wallet integration documentation
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one replacement rule: current Bitcoin Core policy supports limited 1P1C-style replacement of conflicting in-mempool transaction packages when package-level fee, incremental relay, cluster, and feerate-improvement conditions are met. It is narrower than ordinary RBF, package relay, cluster mempool, and submitpackage as an RPC operation.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Ordinary RBF supplies the direct replacement-policy model. Package relay describes propagation, cluster mempool describes internal package accounting, submitpackage describes local submission, and transaction pinning is a motivating risk rather than a technical requirement for understanding package replacement rules.
concepts.notes: Added after deduping against protocol.rbf, dev.submitpackage-rpc, protocol.package-relay, protocol.cluster-mempool, and transaction-pinning concepts. Current policy scope is limited 1P1C-style package replacement rather than unrestricted package RBF.

concepts.title: getmempoolcluster RPC
concepts.sourceContext: getmempoolcluster RPC reference, Bitcoin Core 31.0 cluster-mempool release notes, and mempool terminology
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a concrete diagnostic RPC over the existing cluster-mempool model, not an independent learner-facing prerequisite. The connected-cluster, chunk, fee, and weight ideas belong in protocol.cluster-mempool; the command itself is an optional developer reference.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Merge into protocol.cluster-mempool rather than create a wrapper node: the model is the learning outcome, while submitpackage is a local admission operation, package RBF is replacement policy, and package relay is network propagation.
concepts.notes: Do not add a standalone node. Preserve the RPC as an optional resource on protocol.cluster-mempool for developers who need local diagnostics.

concepts.title: getmempoolfeeratediagram RPC
concepts.sourceContext: Bitcoin Core 31.0 release notes, hidden getmempoolfeeratediagram RPC implementation, and feerate-diagram mempool implementation
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a concrete diagnostic RPC over the existing cluster-mempool and fee-ordering model, not an independent learner-facing prerequisite. The cumulative fee and sigops-adjusted weight curve can enrich protocol.cluster-mempool, while the command itself remains an optional developer reference.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Merge into protocol.cluster-mempool rather than create a wrapper node: the cluster-mempool ordering model is the learning outcome, while per-cluster inspection is a sibling workflow, package RBF is a downstream consumer, and mining-template or eviction nodes are separate policy consumers.
concepts.notes: Do not add a standalone node. Fold the underlying fee-curve vocabulary into protocol.cluster-mempool if needed; keep the hidden RPC as an optional developer reference rather than a graph prerequisite.

concepts.title: Mempool Cluster Limits
concepts.sourceContext: Bitcoin Core 31.0 cluster policy limits, configuration, and Optech cluster-mempool explanation
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one operational policy concept: bounded transaction count and aggregate virtual size for each connected mempool cluster. It is distinct from the cluster-mempool data model, package relay, Package RBF, CPFP, TRUC, and generic mempool policy because it explains the concrete limits that keep package and chunk computation tractable.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The cluster-mempool model is the direct prerequisite for understanding why connected components need bounded size. Mempool policy is inherited through that node; package relay, Package RBF, CPFP, and TRUC are affected workflows or neighboring mitigations rather than direct prerequisites.
concepts.notes: Added after deduping against protocol.cluster-mempool, ops.mempool-policy, protocol.package-relay, protocol.package-rbf, protocol.cpfp, and v3 transaction relay. This is a noun-level policy concept rather than an API wrapper.

concepts.title: Lightning DNS Seeds
concepts.sourceContext: BOLT 10: DNS Bootstrap and Assisted Node Location
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one Lightning discovery mechanism: DNS-assisted bootstrap and known-peer location using Lightning-specific realms, address-type filters, node-ID queries, and DNS reply records. It is narrower than generic DNS seeding and distinct from gossip's ongoing topology dissemination.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Generic DNS seeds supply the bootstrap model, while Lightning gossip supplies the network-specific node-discovery context that BOLT 10 supplements. Peer discovery and routing are already inherited through those parents and are not additional direct prerequisites.
concepts.notes: Added after deduping against ops.dns-seeds, lightning.gossip-protocol, peer-discovery, and routing concepts. This is a Lightning protocol mechanism, not a function/API wrapper.
